topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The string quartet was playing a saccharine waltz, a sound that seemed to grate against the tightness in my chest. I was nursing a lukewarm sparkling water, trying to focus on the elaborate floral arrangements, when I saw him. He was standing near the buffet table, laughing at something one of the groomsmen said, and the sight of him—so polished, so effortlessly happy—hit me like a physical jolt. My hand tightened around the glass until my knuckles were white. Every muscle in my neck seemed to lock up. I forced myself to look away, focusing instead on the pattern of spilled champagne on the white tablecloth. A wave of heat crept up my neck, making the fine hairs stand on end. I needed air, badly, but the thought of navigating the crowded room felt monumental.

[story 2]

"Oh, look who it is!" Sarah’s voice cut through the ambient chatter, bright and loud. I turned, and there he was, leaning against a pillar, looking entirely too relaxed in that tailored suit. The air around him seemed to shift, pulling the surrounding noise into a dull background hum. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I felt a sudden, desperate urge to melt into the wallpaper. I managed a tight smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes. When he finally looked my way, there was a flicker—a brief recognition that felt like an electrical short—before he offered a polite, distant nod. I gripped my clutch bag so hard I could feel the seams digging into my palm, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

I was pretending to inspect the centerpiece arrangements, my gaze darting everywhere but the main entrance. Then, there he was. Standing by the bar, engaged in conversation with an older relative, his posture impeccable, his smile practiced. The music seemed to fade into a low thrumming beneath the surface of my awareness. A strange coldness settled over my skin, despite the warm evening. I stopped mid-stride, my feet feeling rooted to the marble floor. I could feel the blood draining from my face. When our eyes met across the room, for just a second, the practiced ease vanished. There was a pause, a shared acknowledgment of history that made the space between us feel impossibly wide and charged. I quickly looked down at my shoes, focusing intently on the intricate stitching, desperate to become invisible.
